role_hints.oews_basis: 17-1011 Architects, Except Landscape and Naval 5.82% of the 4-digit 5413 wage bill — but 5413 is engineering-dominated (17-2051 Civil Engineers 12.8% reflects 541330, not this code); within 541310 proper, licensed architects plus principals are plausibly ~35-45% of wages (hypothesis; principal draws partly outside wages)
role_hints.note: Stamp, code responsibility, client trust and design judgment stay human; their production hours (drawings, specs, code checking) are heavily AI-exposed. Split the within-role fraction by phase: schematic design and client work low, construction-document production high.

role_hints.oews_basis: 17-3011 Architectural and Civil Drafters 3.28% at 4-digit; in architecture offices proper plausibly ~15-20% of wages (hypothesis)
role_hints.note: Construction-document production — the largest fee phase — is the most directly automatable work in the firm; generative BIM/permit-set tooling targets exactly this.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 6.46% at 4-digit; hypothesis ~10% here
role_hints.note: Consultant coordination and construction administration (site visits, submittals, RFIs) persist in the human/relationship half; the paperwork half automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.72% + 11-9041 Architectural and Engineering Managers 5.53% at 4-digit; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: Client relationships and firm leadership persist; internal reporting automates.

role_hints.role: Interior designers, planners, spec writers, visualization staff
role_hints.note: Renderings and spec assembly are already AI-commoditized; client taste-making and FF&E relationships are not.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the 5413 top-10; long tail, est. remainder ~12-15%
role_hints.note: Proposals, qualifications packages, scheduling and invoicing are heavily automatable.

source_hints.source: Deltek Clarity Architecture & Engineering Industry Study (annual)
source_hints.why: The standard A&E operations benchmark with AI-adoption chapters — usable for current_adoption_pct; covers architecture firms alongside engineering.

source_hints.source: AIA Firm Survey Report and AIA technology/AI publications
source_hints.why: Trade-body primary data on architecture-firm operations, sector mix and technology use.
Demand-cycle evidence — needed to separate cyclical fee pressure from AI-driven deflation.

source_hints.source: AIA Firm Survey fee-structure data (percentage-of-construction-cost vs. fixed fee vs. hourly mix)
source_hints.why: The billing-structure evidence for the capture question.

source_hints.source: Zweig Group ownership-transition and salary surveys; NCARB by the Numbers (annual)
source_hints.why: Principal age and transition data for owners_60plus_pct; NCARB gives licensee demographics.

source_hints.source: Morrissey Goodale AE M&A deal data and commentary
source_hints.why: Architecture bolt-ons within the A&E consolidation wave — deal counts for active_consolidators.

source_hints.source: Zweig Group Valuation Report; Rusk O'Brien Gido + Partners A/E valuation studies
source_hints.why: Small A/E firm multiples — normalize: often quoted pre-owner-comp.
Design-build growth shifts the client relationship to contractors — the structural capture threat.

capture_questions: Architecture fees are commonly a percentage of construction cost or a lump sum benchmarked to it, with construction-document production the largest phase: when AI collapses CD production hours, does the percentage-of-cost convention let firms keep the spread as margin — or do owners and design-builders renegotiate fee percentages downward once production cost visibly drops, and how fast has that repricing happened in past productivity shifts (CAD, BIM)?

Do owners/developers hire the architect for design judgment and brand, or only because permitting requires a stamped set — and how much of the fee sits in automatable production phases versus relationship phases (SD, CA)?
Does design-build growth shift the client relationship to contractors, reducing architects to subcontracted stamp providers with fee-taker economics?

In residential and light-commercial work that is often exempt from architect requirements, does AI design-to-permit tooling bypass architects entirely — and what share of small-firm revenue sits in those exempt segments?
terminal_value_question: If AI can generate code-compliant permit sets, architecture bifurcates into a liability/stamp utility plus a taste-and-brand layer at the high end. The deciding mechanism is the permit counter: do state licensure laws and building departments keep requiring a licensed architect in responsible charge for the building types small firms serve (noting broad residential exemptions), preserving a toll even as production fees deflate — or does the exempt-plus-plan-stamp perimeter expand until the paid product shrinks to judgment and liability at a fraction of today's fee pool? Distinguish AI cutting the firm's production cost from AI shrinking the stamped-set product the firm sells.
special_notes: Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5413 and engineering-contaminated — Civil Engineers (12.8%) outrank Architects (5.82%) only because 541330 dwarfs 541310 at the parent level; rebuild shares for an architecture office and say so.

Pricing-model deflation candidate: percentage-of-construction-cost and lump-sum fees are anchored to historical effort norms — past tooling shifts (CAD, BIM) eventually repriced fees downward; model pi_dist on that precedent rather than assuming the percentage convention protects margin.

Ownership restrictions: several states restrict architecture-firm ownership to licensed professionals (New York notably strict; rules vary widely) — check the state mix before assuming clean PE buyouts; A&E consolidators do buy architecture bolt-ons, so structures exist.

EBITDA normalization: principal comp inside small-firm profit; Zweig-style multiples often pre-owner-comp — normalize before comparing (rule 3). ABI-tracked cyclicality is severe; do not score off a single boom or bust year.
Residential and interiors segments sit partially outside licensure — different exposure profile within the same code; note the mix rather than applying one fraction.

Boundary confusion: engineering is 541330, landscape architecture 541320, drafting services 541340, interior design 541410, building inspection 541350 — A&E surveys (Deltek, Zweig, Morrissey Goodale) span the group; check whose members a benchmark covers before quoting it for architecture alone.
